params.text:
What we mean by safeguarding adults

Safeguarding means protecting an adult's right to live in safety, free from abuse and neglect. As a frontline care worker you are often the person who spends the most time with the people you support, so you are frequently the first to notice that something is wrong. That closeness makes safeguarding a core part of your everyday role, not an occasional extra task.

The Care Act 2014 sets out the legal framework for adult safeguarding in England. It places duties on local authorities and expects everyone who works in care to understand their responsibilities. A person is entitled to a safeguarding response where they:

  • have needs for care and support (whether or not the local authority is meeting them);
  • are experiencing, or at risk of, abuse or neglect; and
  • as a result of those care and support needs are unable to protect themselves from that abuse or neglect.

This is often called the section 42 duty, and when these three points apply the local authority must make (or arrange) enquiries.

params.alt: The six safeguarding principles around a shield

params.text:
Your responsibility as a care worker

You are not expected to investigate abuse or to decide whether it definitely happened. Your job is to recognise the signs, respond safely, record what you have seen or been told, and report your concern to the right person without delay. Doing nothing because you are unsure is never the safe option. If you have a concern, it must always be passed on.

params.question: As a care worker, it is your job to investigate the abuse yourself and decide whether it really happened before telling anyone.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. You should never try to investigate or confirm abuse yourself. Recognise it, keep people safe, record the facts and report the concern promptly.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Your role is to recognise, respond, record and report. Investigating is for the local authority and other agencies.
